title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Bootstrap confidence intervals depend three elements: cdf BB bootstrap replications ti*t_i^*, =1…Bi=1\ldots B bias-correction number z0=Φ(∑iBI(ti*<t0)/B)z_0 = \Phi(\sum_i^B (t_i^* < t_0) / B ) t0=f(x)t_0=f(x) original estimate acceleration number aa measures rate change σt0\sigma_{t_0} xx, data changes. first two depend bootstrap distribution, generated: parametrically non-parametrically. Package bcaboot aims make construction bootstrap confidence intervals almost automatic. two main functions : bca_nonpar() nonparametric bootstrap bca_par() parametric bootstrap results returned bcaboot objects consistent structure support tidy(), glance(), autoplot() integration tidyverse ecosystem. details Efron Narasimhan (2018) paper. Much theory behind approach can found references Efron (1987), DiCiccio Efron (1992), DiCiccio Efron (1996), Efron Hastie (2016).

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Suppose wish construct bootstrap confidence intervals R2R^2-statistic linear regression. Using diabetes data lars (442 11) example, use function regress y x, matrix 10 predictors, compute R2R^2. Constructing bootstrap confidence intervals involves merely calling bca_nonpar: result prints clean summary:
code: data(diabetes, package = "bcaboot") Xy <- cbind(diabetes$x, diabetes$y) rfun <- function(Xy) {     y <- Xy[, 11]     X <- Xy[, 1:10]     summary(lm(y ~ X) )$adj.r.squared } set.seed(1234) result <- bca_nonpar(x = Xy, B = 2000, func = rfun, verbose = FALSE) result ##  conf.level    bca.lo    bca.hi    std.lo    std.hi ##        0.95 0.4221740 0.5613841 0.4429423 0.5701783 ##        0.90 0.4392968 0.5507219 0.4531704 0.5599503 ##        0.80 0.4541906 0.5387434 0.4649627 0.5481579 ##        0.68 0.4647324 0.5298461 0.4742814 0.5388392

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: tidy() method returns tibble one row per (confidence level, method) combination, following broom conventions: glance() method provides one-row summary bootstrap run:
code: tidy(result) ## # A tibble: 8 × 7 ##   conf.level method   estimate conf.low conf.high jacksd.low jacksd.high ##        <dbl> <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>       <dbl> ## 1       0.95 bca         0.507    0.422     0.561    0.00776     0.00201 ## 2       0.95 standard    0.507    0.443     0.570   NA          NA       ## 3       0.9  bca         0.507    0.439     0.551    0.00280     0.00173 ## 4       0.9  standard    0.507    0.453     0.560   NA          NA       ## 5       0.8  bca         0.507    0.454     0.539    0.00262     0.00157 ## 6       0.8  standard    0.507    0.465     0.548   NA          NA       ## 7       0.68 bca         0.507    0.465     0.530    0.00143     0.00158 ## 8       0.68 standard    0.507    0.474     0.539   NA          NA glance(result) ## # A tibble: 1 × 9 ##   method accel      theta sdboot     z0        a sdjack     B boot_mean ##   <chr>  <chr>      <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>    <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl> ## 1 nonpar regression 0.507 0.0325 -0.268 -0.00585 0.0323  2000     0.515

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: ggplot2 available, autoplot() produces publication-ready plot confidence intervals:
code: library(ggplot2) autoplot(result)

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: accel argument controls acceleration aa estimated: accel = "regression" (default): uses local regression bootstrap count vectors nearest uniform. Also computes gbca diagnostic. accel = "jackknife": uses classical delete-one (delete-group) jackknife influence values.
code: set.seed(1234) result_jk <- bca_nonpar(x = Xy, B = 2000, func = rfun,                         accel = "jackknife", verbose = FALSE) tidy(result_jk) ## # A tibble: 8 × 7 ##   conf.level method   estimate conf.low conf.high jacksd.low jacksd.high ##        <dbl> <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>       <dbl> ## 1       0.95 bca         0.507    0.435     0.560    0.00412     0.00155 ## 2       0.95 standard    0.507    0.443     0.570   NA          NA       ## 3       0.9  bca         0.507    0.443     0.551    0.00497     0.00224 ## 4       0.9  standard    0.507    0.453     0.560   NA          NA       ## 5       0.8  bca         0.507    0.454     0.540    0.00127     0.00158 ## 6       0.8  standard    0.507    0.465     0.548   NA          NA       ## 7       0.68 bca         0.507    0.464     0.531    0.00186     0.00177 ## 8       0.68 standard    0.507    0.474     0.539   NA          NA

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: logistic regression fit data 812 neonates large clinic. summary dataset. goal predict death versus survival—yy 1 0, respectively—basis 11 baseline variables one resp particular concern. (207 deaths 605 survivors.) θ\theta, parameter interest coefficient resp. Discussions investigator suggested weighting 4 1 deaths versus non-deaths.

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Parametric bootstrapping context requires us independently sample response according estimated probabilities regression model. discussed paper accompanying software, routine bca_par also requires sufficient statistics β̂=M′y\hat{\beta} = M^\prime y MM model matrix. Therefore, makes sense function work. function glm_boot returns list estimate θ̂\hat{\theta}, bootstrap estimates, sufficient statistics. Now can compute bootstrap estimates using bca_par. can examine results using tidy methods: bootstrap standard error using B=2000B=2000 samples resp can read glance output:
code: weights <- with(neonates, ifelse(y == 0, 1, 4)) glm_model <- glm(formula = y ~ ., family = "binomial", weights = weights, data = neonates) summary(glm_model) ##  ## Call: ## glm(formula = y ~ ., family = "binomial", data = neonates, weights = weights) ##  ## Coefficients: ##             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)     ## (Intercept) -0.26510    0.07598  -3.489 0.000485 *** ## gest        -0.70602    0.13117  -5.383 7.35e-08 *** ## ap          -0.78594    0.07874  -9.982  < 2e-16 *** ## bwei        -0.23332    0.12592  -1.853 0.063879 .   ## gen         -0.04107    0.07355  -0.558 0.576594     ## resp         0.94306    0.08974  10.509  < 2e-16 *** ## head         0.04813    0.08057   0.597 0.550299     ## hr           0.03504    0.07191   0.487 0.626045     ## cpap         0.43438    0.08869   4.898 9.70e-07 *** ## age          0.15727    0.08492   1.852 0.064041 .   ## temp        -0.05960    0.08506  -0.701 0.483520     ## size        -0.37477    0.09919  -3.778 0.000158 *** ## --- ## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ##  ## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1) ##  ##     Null deviance: 1951.7  on 811  degrees of freedom ## Residual deviance: 1187.2  on 800  degrees of freedom ## AIC: 1211.2 ##  ## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5 glm_boot <- function(B, glm_model, weights, var = "resp") {     pi_hat <- glm_model$fitted.values     n <- length(pi_hat)     y_star <- sapply(seq_len(B), function(i) ifelse(runif(n) <= pi_hat, 1, 0))     beta_star <- apply(y_star, 2, function(y) {         boot_data <- glm_model$data         boot_data$y <- y         coef(glm(formula = y ~ ., data = boot_data, weights = weights, family = "binomial"))     })     list(theta = coef(glm_model)[var],          theta_star = beta_star[var, ],          suff_stat = t(y_star) %*% model.matrix(glm_model)) } set.seed(3891) glm_boot_out <- glm_boot(B = 2000, glm_model = glm_model, weights = weights) glm_bca <- bca_par(t0 = glm_boot_out$theta,                    tt = glm_boot_out$theta_star,                    bb = glm_boot_out$suff_stat) glm_bca ##  conf.level    bca.lo   bca.hi    std.lo   std.hi ##        0.95 0.5981733 1.213676 0.6394385 1.246691 ##        0.90 0.6549470 1.167789 0.6882535 1.197876 ##        0.80 0.7169529 1.113660 0.7445341 1.141595 ##        0.68 0.7635391 1.067160 0.7890090 1.097120 tidy(glm_bca) ## # A tibble: 8 × 7 ##   conf.level method   estimate conf.low conf.high jacksd.low jacksd.high ##        <dbl> <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>       <dbl> ## 1       0.95 bca         0.943    0.598      1.21    0.0247      0.0125  ## 2       0.95 standard    0.943    0.639      1.25   NA          NA       ## 3       0.9  bca         0.943    0.655      1.17    0.0193      0.00848 ## 4       0.9  standard    0.943    0.688      1.20   NA          NA       ## 5       0.8  bca         0.943    0.717      1.11    0.0107      0.00907 ## 6       0.8  standard    0.943    0.745      1.14   NA          NA       ## 7       0.68 bca         0.943    0.764      1.07    0.00676     0.00507 ## 8       0.68 standard    0.943    0.789      1.10   NA          NA glance(glm_bca) ## # A tibble: 1 × 9 ##   method accel theta sdboot     z0       a sdjack     B boot_mean ##   <chr>  <chr> <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl> ## 1 par    NA    0.943  0.155 -0.215 -0.0194     NA  2000     0.982

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Now suppose wish use nonstandard estimation procedure, example, via glmnet package, uses cross-validation figure best fit, corresponding penalization parameter λ\lambda (named lambda.min). can examine estimates lambda.min follows. Following lines , create helper function perform bootstrap. go. can compare models side--side:
code: X <- as.matrix(neonates[, seq_len(11)]) ; Y <- neonates$y; glmnet_model <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(x = X, y = Y, family = "binomial", weights = weights) coefs <- as.matrix(coef(glmnet_model, s = glmnet_model$lambda.min)) knitr::kable(data.frame(variable = rownames(coefs), coefficient = coefs[, 1]), row.names = FALSE, digits = 3) glmnet_boot <- function(B, X, y, glmnet_model, weights, var = "resp") {     lambda <- glmnet_model$lambda.min     theta <- as.matrix(coef(glmnet_model, s = lambda))     pi_hat <- predict(glmnet_model, newx = X, s = "lambda.min", type = "response")     n <- length(pi_hat)     y_star <- sapply(seq_len(B), function(i) ifelse(runif(n) <= pi_hat, 1, 0))     beta_star <- apply(y_star, 2,                        function(y) {                            as.matrix(coef(glmnet::glmnet(x = X, y = y, lambda = lambda, weights = weights, family = "binomial")))                        })      rownames(beta_star) <- rownames(theta)     list(theta = theta[var, ],          theta_star = beta_star[var, ],          suff_stat = t(y_star) %*% X) } glmnet_boot_out <- glmnet_boot(B = 2000, X, y, glmnet_model, weights) glmnet_bca <- bca_par(t0 = glmnet_boot_out$theta,                       tt = glmnet_boot_out$theta_star,                       bb = glmnet_boot_out$suff_stat) autoplot(glm_bca) + ggtitle("glm model") autoplot(glmnet_bca) + ggtitle("glmnet model")

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Assume two independent estimates variance normal theory: σ̂12∼σ12χn12n1, \hat{\sigma}_1^2\sim\frac{\sigma_1^2\chi_{n_1}^2}{n_1}, σ̂22∼σ22χn22n2. \hat{\sigma}_2^2\sim\frac{\sigma_2^2\chi_{n_2}^2}{n_2}. Suppose now parameter interest θ=σ12σ22     \theta=\frac{\sigma_1^2}{\sigma_2^2} wish compute confidence limits. setting, theory yields exact limits: θ̂(α)=θ̂Fn1,n21−α. \hat{\theta}(\alpha) = \frac{\hat{\theta}}{F_{n_1,n_2}^{1-\alpha}}. can apply bca_par problem. , helper functions. Note additional function funcF corresponds τ(β̂*)\tau(\hat{\beta}^*) paper. function expressing parameter interest function sample. tidy output shows BCa, standard, ABC limits: can compare exact F-distribution limits: Clearly BCa limits match exact values well suggests large upward correction standard limits.
code: ratio_boot <- function(B, v1, v2) {     s1 <- sqrt(v1) * rchisq(n = B, df = n1)  / n1     s2 <- sqrt(v2) * rchisq(n = B, df = n2)  / n2     theta_star <- s1 / s2     beta_star <- cbind(s1, s2)     list(theta = v1 / v2,          theta_star = theta_star,          suff_stat = beta_star) }  funcF <- function(beta) {     beta[1] / beta[2] } B <- 16000; n1 <- 10; n2 <- 42 ratio_boot_out <- ratio_boot(B, 1, 1) ratio_bca <- bca_par(t0 = ratio_boot_out$theta,                      tt = ratio_boot_out$theta_star,                      bb = ratio_boot_out$suff_stat, func = funcF) tidy(ratio_bca) ## # A tibble: 12 × 7 ##    conf.level method   estimate conf.low conf.high jacksd.low jacksd.high ##         <dbl> <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>       <dbl> ##  1       0.95 abc             1   0.405       3.42   NA           NA      ##  2       0.95 bca             1   0.412       3.14    0.00824      0.0957 ##  3       0.95 standard        1  -0.0527      2.05   NA           NA      ##  4       0.9  abc             1   0.472       2.73   NA           NA      ##  5       0.9  bca             1   0.475       2.57    0.00743      0.0426 ##  6       0.9  standard        1   0.117       1.88   NA           NA      ##  7       0.8  abc             1   0.562       2.15   NA           NA      ##  8       0.8  bca             1   0.559       2.09    0.00687      0.0316 ##  9       0.8  standard        1   0.312       1.69   NA           NA      ## 10       0.68 abc             1   0.646       1.81   NA           NA      ## 11       0.68 bca             1   0.639       1.77    0.00635      0.0247 ## 12       0.68 standard        1   0.466       1.53   NA           NA exact <- 1 / qf(df1 = n1, df2 = n2, p = 1 - as.numeric(rownames(ratio_bca$limits))) knitr::kable(cbind(ratio_bca$limits, exact = exact), digits = 3) autoplot(ratio_bca)

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Version 1.0 introduces new API. old functions continue work emit deprecation warnings. migrate:

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: new objects use $limits ($lims), $B_mean ($B.mean), $stats always 2-row matrix (never named vector). Use tidy() glance() instead accessing list components directly:
code: ## Old way result$lims[, "bca"] result$stats["theta"]  ## New way tidy(result)    # tibble of confidence limits glance(result)  # tibble of summary statistics

title: Authors and Citation
text: Bradley Efron. Author. Balasubramanian Narasimhan. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Efron B, Narasimhan B (2026). bcaboot: Bias Corrected Bootstrap Confidence Intervals. R package version 1.0, https://bnaras.github.io/bcaboot/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {bcaboot: Bias Corrected Bootstrap Confidence Intervals},   author = {Bradley Efron and Balasubramanian Narasimhan},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 1.0},   url = {https://bnaras.github.io/bcaboot/}, }

title: Bias Corrected Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Bias-corrected accelerated (BCa) bootstrap confidence intervals, computed almost automatically. package provides two main functions: bca_nonpar() nonparametric bootstrap bca_par() parametric bootstrap Results integrate tidyverse via tidy(), glance(), autoplot() methods.

title: Bias Corrected Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
code: # From CRAN install.packages("bcaboot")  # Development version # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("bnaras/bcaboot")

title: Bias Corrected Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
code: library(bcaboot)  data(diabetes) Xy <- cbind(diabetes$x, diabetes$y) rfun <- function(Xy) {     y <- Xy[, 11]; X <- Xy[, 1:10]     summary(lm(y ~ X))$adj.r.squared }  set.seed(1234) result <- bca_nonpar(Xy, B = 2000, func = rfun, verbose = FALSE) result ## BCa Bootstrap Confidence Intervals ##   Method: nonpar (regression acceleration) ##   B = 2000, theta = 0.5065603, sdboot = 0.03245877 ##  ## Confidence limits: ##  conf.level    bca.lo    bca.hi    std.lo    std.hi ##        0.95 0.4221740 0.5613841 0.4429423 0.5701783 ##        0.90 0.4392968 0.5507219 0.4531704 0.5599503 ##        0.80 0.4541906 0.5387434 0.4649627 0.5481579 ##        0.68 0.4647324 0.5298461 0.4742814 0.5388392 ##  ## Diagnostics: ##   z0 = -0.2676094, a = -0.005846673, sdjack = 0.03232833 tidy(result) ## # A tibble: 8 × 7 ##   conf.level method   estimate conf.low conf.high jacksd.low jacksd.high ##        <dbl> <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>       <dbl> ## 1       0.95 bca         0.507    0.422     0.561    0.00776     0.00201 ## 2       0.95 standard    0.507    0.443     0.570   NA          NA       ## 3       0.9  bca         0.507    0.439     0.551    0.00280     0.00173 ## 4       0.9  standard    0.507    0.453     0.560   NA          NA       ## 5       0.8  bca         0.507    0.454     0.539    0.00262     0.00157 ## 6       0.8  standard    0.507    0.465     0.548   NA          NA       ## 7       0.68 bca         0.507    0.465     0.530    0.00143     0.00158 ## 8       0.68 standard    0.507    0.474     0.539   NA          NA library(ggplot2) autoplot(result)

title: Bias Corrected Bootstrap Confidence Intervals

what: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals — bcaboot
title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals — bcaboot
text: Bootstrap confidence intervals depend three elements: () cumulative distribution bootstrap replications, (b) bias-correction, (c) acceleration number measures rate change standard deviation estimate data changes.  first two depend bootstrap distribution, generated: parametrically non-parametrically. Therefore, difference parametric bca analysis lie nonparametric estimation acceleration, often negligible error.

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals — bcaboot
text: Maintainer: Balasubramanian Narasimhan naras@stat.stanford.edu Authors: Bradley Efron brad@stat.stanford.edu

what: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack
title: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack
text: routine computes nonparametric confidence intervals bootstrap estimates. reproducibility, save set random number state calling routine.

title: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack
code: bcajack(   x,   B,   func,   ...,   m = nrow(x),   mr = 5,   K = 2,   J = 10,   alpha = c(0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.16),   verbose = TRUE )

title: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack
text: x \(n \times p\) data matrix, rows observed \(p\)-vectors, assumed independently sampled target population. \(p\) 1 x can vector. B number bootstrap replications. can also vector B bootstrap replications estimated parameter interest, computed separately. func function \(\hat{\theta}=func(x)\) computing estimate parameter interest; \(func(x)\) return real value \(n^\prime \times p\) matrix \(x^\prime\), \(n^\prime\) necessarily equal \(n\) ... additional arguments func. m integer less equal \(n\); routine collects \(n\) rows x m groups speed jackknife calculations estimating acceleration value \(\); typically m 20 40 exactly divide \(n\). However, warnings shown. mr \(m < n\) mr repetions randomly grouped jackknife calculations averaged. K non-negative integer. K > 0, bcajack also returns estimates internal standard error, , variability due stopping B bootstrap replications rather going infinity. obtained second type jackknifing, taking average K separate jackknife estimates, randomly splitting B bootstrap replications J groups. J number groups bootstrap replications split alpha percentiles desired bca confidence limits. One needs provide alpha values 0.5; upper limits automatically computed verbose logical verbose progress messages

title: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack
text: named list several items lims : first column shows estimated bca confidence limits requested alpha percentiles. can compared standard limits \(\hat{\theta} +     \hat{\sigma}z_{\alpha}\), third column. second column jacksd gives internal standard errors bca limits, quite small example. Column 4, pct, gives percentiles ordered B bootstrap replications corresponding bca limits, eg 897th largest replication equalling .975 bca limit .557. stats : top line stats shows 5 estimates: theta \(f(x)\), original point estimate parameter interest; sdboot bootstrap estimate standard error; z0 bca bias correction value, case quite negative; acceleration, component bca limits (nearly zero ); sdjack jackknife estimate standard error theta. Bottom line gives internal standard errors five quantities . substantial z0 . B.mean : bootstrap sample size B, mean B bootstrap replications \(\hat{\theta^*}\) ustats : bias-corrected estimator 2 * t0 - mean(tt), estimate sdu sampling error seed : random number state reproducibility

title: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack
text: Bootstrap confidence intervals depend three elements: cdf \(B\) bootstrap replications \(t_i^*\), \(=1\ldots B\) bias-correction number \(z_0=\Phi(\sum_i^B (t_i^* < t_0) / B )\) \(t_0=f(x)\) original estimate acceleration number \(\) measures rate change \(\sigma_{t_0}\) \(x\), data changes. first two depend bootstrap distribution, generated: parametrically non-parametrically. Program bcajack can used hybrid fashion vector tt B bootstrap replications first generated parametric model. , diabetes example , might first draw bootstrap samples \(y^* \sim N(X\hat{\beta}, \hat{\sigma}^2 )\) \(\hat{\beta}\) \(\hat{\sigma}\) obtained lm(y~X); \(y^*\) provide bootstrap replication tstar = rfun(cbind(X, ystar)).  get bca intervals bcajack(Xy, tt, rfun ....) tt, vector B tstar values. difference full parametric bca analysis lie nonparametric estimation \(\), often negligible error.

title: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack
text: DiCiccio T Efron B (1996). Bootstrap confidence intervals. Statistical Science 11, 189-228 Efron B (1987). Better bootstrap confidence intervals. JASA 82 171-200 B. Efron B. Narasimhan. Automatic Construction Bootstrap Confidence Intervals, 2018.

title: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack
code: data(diabetes, package = "bcaboot") Xy <- cbind(diabetes$x, diabetes$y) rfun <- function(Xy) {   y <- Xy[, 11]   X <- Xy[, 1:10]   summary(lm(y~X) )$adj.r.squared } set.seed(1234) ## n = 442 = 34 * 13 bcajack(x = Xy, B = 1000, func = rfun, m = 34, verbose = FALSE) #> Warning: `bcajack()` was deprecated in bcaboot 1.0. #> ℹ Please use `bca_nonpar()` instead. #>  #> ── BCa Bootstrap Confidence Intervals  #> Method: nonpar (jackknife acceleration) #> B = 1000, theta = 0.5065603, sdboot = 0.03284722 #>  #> Confidence limits: #>  conf.level    bca.lo    bca.hi    std.lo    std.hi #>        0.95 0.4238753 0.5582875 0.4421809 0.5709397 #>        0.90 0.4342607 0.5496734 0.4525314 0.5605892 #>        0.80 0.4513913 0.5388794 0.4644649 0.5486557 #>        0.68 0.4617252 0.5305165 0.4738951 0.5392255 #>  #> Diagnostics: #> z0 = -0.26112, a = -0.01143494, sdjack = 0.02985439

what: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack2
title: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack2
text: function version bcajack allows recomputations original statistic function \(f\) carried separately. advantage \(f\) time-consuming, case B replications nonparametric bca calculations might need done distributed basis. use bcajack2 mode, first compute list Blist via Blist <- list(Y = Y, tt = tt, t0 = t0).  tt vector length B -th entry tt[] <- func(x[Ii,], ...), x \(n \times p\) data matrix Ii bootstrap vector (observation) indices. Y B \(n\) count matrix, whose -th row counts corresponding Ii. example n = 5 Ii = (2, 5, 2, 1, 4), Yi = (1, 2, 0, 1, 1). computed Blist, bcajack2 invoked bcajack2(Blist) without need enter function \(func\).

title: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack2
code: bcajack2(   x,   B,   func,   ...,   m = NULL,   mr,   pct = 0.333,   K = 2,   J = 12,   alpha = c(0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.16),   verbose = TRUE )

title: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack2
text: x \(n \times p\) data matrix, rows observed \(p\)-vectors, assumed independently sampled target population. \(p\) 1 x can vector. B number bootstrap replications. B can also vector B bootstrap replications estimated parameter interest, computed separately. B Blist explained , x needed. func function \(\hat{\theta}=func(x)\) computing estimate parameter interest; \(func(x)\) return real value \(n^\prime \times p\) matrix \(x^\prime\), \(n^\prime\) necessarily equal \(n\) ... additional arguments func. m integer less equal \(n\); routine collects \(n\) rows x m groups speed jackknife calculations estimating acceleration value \(\); typically m 20 40 exactly divide \(n\). However, warnings shown. mr \(m < n\) mr repetions randomly grouped jackknife calculations averaged. pct bcajack2 uses count vectors nearest (1,1,...1) estimate gradient statistic, "nearest" defined count vectors smallest pct B . Default value `pct 1/3 (see appendix Efron Narasimhan details) K non-negative integer. K > 0, bcajack also returns estimates internal standard error, , variability due stopping B bootstrap replications rather going infinity. obtained second type jackknifing, taking average K separate jackknife estimates, randomly splitting B bootstrap replications J groups. J number groups bootstrap replications split alpha percentiles desired bca confidence limits. One needs provide alpha values 0.5; upper limits automatically computed verbose logical verbose progress messages

title: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack2
text: named list several items lims : first column shows estimated bca confidence limits requested alpha percentiles. can compared standard limits \(\hat{\theta} +     \hat{\sigma}z_{\alpha}\), third column. second column jacksd gives internal standard errors bca limits, quite small example. Column 4, pct, gives percentiles ordered B bootstrap replications corresponding bca limits, eg 897th largest replication equalling .975 bca limit .557. stats : top line stats shows 5 estimates: theta \(func(x)\), original point estimate parameter interest; sdboot bootstrap estimate standard error; z0 bca bias correction value, case quite negative; acceleration, component bca limits (nearly zero ); sdjack jackknife estimate standard error theta. Bottom line gives internal standard errors five quantities . substantial z0 . B.mean : bootstrap sample size B, mean B bootstrap replications \(\hat{\theta^*}\) ustats : bias-corrected estimator 2 * t0 - mean(tt), estimate sdu sampling error seed : random number state reproducibility

title: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack2
code: data(diabetes, package = "bcaboot") Xy <- cbind(diabetes$x, diabetes$y) rfun <- function(Xy) {   y <- Xy[, 11]   X <- Xy[, 1:10]   summary(lm(y~X) )$adj.r.squared } set.seed(1234) bcajack2(x = Xy, B = 1000, func = rfun, m = 40, verbose = FALSE) #> Warning: `bcajack2()` was deprecated in bcaboot 1.0. #> ℹ Please use `bca_nonpar()` instead. #>  #> ── BCa Bootstrap Confidence Intervals  #> Method: nonpar (regression acceleration) #> B = 1000, theta = 0.5065603, sdboot = 0.03076788 #>  #> Confidence limits: #>  conf.level    bca.lo    bca.hi    std.lo    std.hi #>        0.95 0.4289798 0.5540292 0.4462564 0.5668642 #>        0.90 0.4374506 0.5459683 0.4559517 0.5571690 #>        0.80 0.4533929 0.5357038 0.4671297 0.5459909 #>        0.68 0.4621266 0.5255210 0.4759630 0.5371577 #>  #> Diagnostics: #> z0 = -0.3853205, a = -0.01094242, sdjack = 0.03139928

what: Compute nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bcanon
title: Compute nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bcanon
text: function computes nonparametric BCa confidence intervals using regression bootstrap count vectors (approach bcajack2) plus generalized BCa (gbca) diagnostic tests adequacy BCa approximation via warped normal comparison. Efron (1987) Sec 7.

title: Compute nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bcanon
code: bcanon(   B,   x,   func,   ...,   m = nrow(x),   pct = 0.333,   K = 2,   J = 12,   alpha = c(0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.16),   verbose = TRUE )

title: Compute nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bcanon
text: B number bootstrap replications, list components Y (count matrix), tt (bootstrap replicates), t0 (original estimate) x data matrix dimension \(n\times p\), rows assumed mutually independent func R function, \(func(x, \ldots)\) statistic interest ... additional arguments func m number units; \(m<n\) original units collected groups size \(n/m\); useful \(n\) large pct proportion nearby count vectors used finding gradient K number jackknife repetitions internal standard error estimation J number jackknife folds per repetition alpha percentiles coverage probabilities bootstrap intervals verbose logical verbose progress messages

title: Compute nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bcanon
text: named list class bcaboot containing: call: matched call lims : BCa confidence limits (bca), jackknife internal standard errors (jacksd), standard limits (std), bootstrap percentiles (pct) stats : Estimates jackknife Monte Carlo errors theta, sdboot, sdjack, z0, , plus big-acceleration SE stability (, sese) B.mean : bootstrap sample size B mean replications equiv : gbca coverage estimates showing alpha vs alphatilda (coverage-adjusted alpha); BCa exact equal. Also jackknife Monte Carlo standard deviations. seed : random number state reproducibility

what: Compute parametric bootstrap confidence intervals — bcapar
title: Compute parametric bootstrap confidence intervals — bcapar
text: bcapar computes parametric bootstrap confidence intervals real-valued parameter theta p-parameter exponential family. described Section 4 reference .

title: Compute parametric bootstrap confidence intervals — bcapar
Compute parametric bootstrap confidence intervals — bcapar
text: t0 Observed estimate theta, usually maximum likelihood. tt vector parametric bootstrap replications theta length B, usually large, say B = 2000 bb B p matrix natural sufficient vectors, p dimension exponential family. alpha percentiles desired bca confidence limits. One needs provide alpha values 0.5; upper limits automatically computed J, K Parameters controlling jackknife estimates Monte Carlo error: J jackknife folds, jackknife standard errors averaged K random divisions bb trun Truncation parameter used calculation acceleration . pct Proportion "nearby" b vectors used calculation t., gradient vector theta. cd cd 1 bca confidence density also returned; see Section 11.6 reference Efron Hastie (2016) func Function \(\hat{\theta} = func(b)\). missing output includes abc estimates; see reference DiCiccio Efron (1992)

title: Compute parametric bootstrap confidence intervals — bcapar
text: named list several items: lims : Bca confidence limits (first column) standard limits (fourth column). Also abc limits (fifth column) func provided. second column, jacksd, jackknife estimates Monte Carlo error; pct, third column proportion replicates tt less bcalim value stats : Estimates jackknife Monte Carlo errors: theta = \(\hat{\theta}\); sd, bootstrap standard deviation \(\hat{\theta}\); acceleration estimate; az another acceleration estimate depends less extreme values tt; z0 bias-correction estimate; big-measure raw acceleration; sdd delta method estimate standard deviation \(\hat{\theta}\); mean average tt abcstats : abc estimates z0, returned func provided ustats : bias-corrected estimator 2 * t0 - mean(tt). ustats gives ustat, estimate sdu sampling error, jackknife estimates monte carlo error ustat sdu. Also given B, number bootstrap replications seed : random number state reproducibility

title: Compute parametric bootstrap confidence intervals — bcapar
text: DiCiccio T Efron B (1996). Bootstrap confidence intervals. Statistical Science 11, 189-228 T. DiCiccio B. Efron. accurate confidence intervals exponential families. Biometrika (1992) p231-245. Efron B (1987). Better bootstrap confidence intervals. JASA 82, 171-200 B. Efron T. Hastie. Computer Age Statistical Inference. Cambridge University Press, 2016. B. Efron B. Narasimhan. Automatic Construction Bootstrap Confidence Intervals, 2018.

title: Compute parametric bootstrap confidence intervals — bcapar
code: data(diabetes, package = "bcaboot") X <- diabetes$x y <- scale(diabetes$y, center = TRUE, scale = FALSE) lm.model <- lm(y ~ X - 1) mu.hat <- lm.model$fitted.values sigma.hat <- stats::sd(lm.model$residuals) t0 <- summary(lm.model)$adj.r.squared y.star <- sapply(mu.hat, rnorm, n = 1000, sd = sigma.hat) tt <- apply(y.star, 1, function(y) summary(lm(y ~ X - 1))$adj.r.squared) b.star <- y.star %*% X set.seed(1234) bcapar(t0 = t0, tt = tt, bb = b.star) #> Warning: `bcapar()` was deprecated in bcaboot 1.0. #> ℹ Please use `bca_par()` instead. #>  #> ── BCa Bootstrap Confidence Intervals  #> Method: par #> B = 1000, theta = 0.5065862, sdboot = 0.02803423 #>  #> Confidence limits: #>  conf.level    bca.lo    bca.hi    std.lo    std.hi #>        0.95 0.4381990 0.5514613 0.4516401 0.5615323 #>        0.90 0.4450255 0.5423186 0.4604740 0.5526984 #>        0.80 0.4563314 0.5317548 0.4706589 0.5425135 #>        0.68 0.4672079 0.5248057 0.4787074 0.5344651 #>  #> Diagnostics: #> z0 = -0.3584588, a = 0.0019184

what: Plots of bca confidence limits — bcaplot
title: Plots of bca confidence limits — bcaplot
text: bcaplot uses output bcajack, bcajack2, bcanon, bcapar plot bca standard confidence limits parameter interest.

title: Plots of bca confidence limits — bcaplot
code: bcaplot(   vl,   main = " ",   xlab = "coverage",   ylab = "limits",   alpha = c(0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.16),   ylim,   xlim,   add = 0,   sub = "black=bca, green=standard",   sw = 1,   ... )

title: Plots of bca confidence limits — bcaplot
text: vl output bcajack, bcajack2, bcanon, bcapar main main caption (can empty) xlab x axis label (supplied specified) ylab y axis labels (supplied specified) alpha Coverages \(1-2\alpha\), e.g. alpha=c(.025,.05) plots intervals [.025,.975] [.05,.95]. Default alpha=c(.025,.05,.1,.16) giving coverages .95,.90,.80,.68 ylim y axis plot limits set automatically provided xlim x axis plot limits set automatically provided add add=1 adds new plot bca limits (red) existing plot sub subtitle (can empty) sw sw=1 draws light vertical dashed lines showing bca intervals ... args plot

title: Plots of bca confidence limits — bcaplot
text: confidence interval endpoints plotted vertically versus two-sided coverages \(1-2\alpha\). Bca limits black, Standard limits green (dashed.). vl$lims includes column "jacksd" jackknife internal standard deviations indicated vertical red bars centered bca limit points.

title: Compute BCa confidence limits from bootstrap replicates. — compute_bca_limits

text: Named list limits (BCa quantiles), bca_idx (indices sorted boot_reps), iles (adjusted percentile levels).

text: row Y bootstrap count vector. deviance measures far bootstrap sample departs simple random sampling (uniform counts). Limit Yi=0: term equals 2. EN20 appendix.

title: bcaboot 1.0
text: New primary API: bca_nonpar() replaces bcajack(), bcajack2(), bcanon(). bca_par() replaces bcapar(). computation functions now return canonical "bcaboot" object consistent structure: $limits (9x4 matrix), $stats (2x5 matrix), $B_mean, $ustats. old field names ($lims, $B.mean) longer used. User-facing parameter conf.level (e.g., c(0.95, 0.90)) replaces alpha (e.g., c(0.025, 0.05)). Internally, alpha expansion unchanged. Descriptive parameter names: n_groups (m), group_reps (mr), kl_fraction (pct), n_jack (K), jack_groups (J), truncation (trun), conf_density (cd), boot_data (overloaded B). bcanon() removed (never CRAN).

title: bcaboot 1.0
text: bcajack(), bcajack2(), bcapar(), bcaplot() deprecated lifecycle warnings (per session). continue work thin wrappers translate new API.

title: bcaboot 1.0
text: Issue #2: Fixed grouped jackknife (n_groups < n) using sapply(seq_len_m, sample.int, ...) accidentally passed iteration values first positional arg, overriding n= keyword enabling replacement. Replaced matrix(sample.int(n, n-r), nrow=m) correct without-replacement partition. (Reported admash) Issue #7: Fixed dimension drop x single-column matrix (vector input). x[-, ] n x 1 matrix returned vector instead matrix, causing type inconsistency jackknife bootstrap code paths. Added drop = FALSE matrix subsetting passed func. (Reported R180) Issue #4 / #1: regression_accel now checks underdetermined regression (nearby samples < ncol(Y)) errors actionable message instead silently returning NAs. Suggests increasing B, increasing kl_fraction, switching accel = "jackknife". (Reported Tim Pollington, Thomas Covert) PR #8: Fixed missing return() K = 0 early-exit path bcajack(), bcajack2(), bcapar(), caused execution fall K > 0 code. rewrite, early exits use explicit return(new_bcaboot(...)). (Reported Bettina Gruen)

title: bcaboot 1.0
text: tidy() method: returns tibble one row per (confidence level, method) pair, following broom conventions (conf.level, method, estimate, conf.low, conf.high). glance() method: returns one-row tibble summarizing bootstrap run (method, accel, theta, sdboot, z0, , sdjack, B, boot_mean). autoplot() method: ggplot2 visualization BCa vs standard confidence intervals. Requires ggplot2 (Suggests). Improved print(): formatted summary confidence limits table instead raw list dump. bca_nonpar() always computes gbca diagnostic using accel = "regression" (tryCatch robustness). user-facing messages use cli consistent formatting.

title: bcaboot 1.0
text: Added cli, tibble, generics, lifecycle Imports. Added ggplot2 Suggests (autoplot()).

title: bcaboot 1.0
text: Shared helpers extracted R/bca_core.R: expand_alpha(), bootstrap_resample(), jackknife_accel(), regression_accel(), compute_ustats(). new_bcaboot() canonical constructor guarantees consistent return structure across computation functions. Test suite expanded 14 34 tests, including adapter tests proving numerical equivalence pre-1.0 fixtures.
